model_narrative: We schedule a set of jobs on a small number of parallel machines. For each job we choose a start time and we choose which machine processes it, and for each pair of jobs that could land on the same machine we decide which one goes first. Each job occupies its machine for a fixed duration once it starts. The goal is to finish everything as quickly as possible, so we minimize the makespan, the time at which the last job completes.

sets.name: J
sets.doc: the jobs that must be scheduled

sets.name: M
sets.doc: the machines available to process the jobs

sets.name: PAIRS
sets.members: all ordered pairs (j, k) of distinct jobs with j before k in the job ordering
sets.doc: the unordered pairs of distinct jobs, each listed once as (j, k) with j ahead of k in the job ordering; used to reason about the relative sequence of two jobs that might share a machine

params.name: release
params.doc: the release time of each job, the earliest time at which that job is allowed to start, in time units

params.name: duration
params.doc: the processing duration of each job, the number of time units the job occupies a machine once started

params.name: due
params.doc: the due date of each job, the time by which it is ideally completed, in time units

vars.name: start
vars.doc: the start time chosen for each job, bounded below by 0 and above by a large allowable horizon

vars.name: makespan
vars.doc: the makespan of the schedule, meaning the latest completion time over all jobs

vars.name: early
vars.doc: the earliness of each job, the amount of time by which the job completes ahead of its due date

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the machine assignment indicator, equal to 1 when the job is assigned to that machine and 0 otherwise

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the sequencing indicator for a pair of jobs, equal to 1 when the first job of the pair is scheduled before the second job of the pair, and 0 when the order is reversed

description: Each job cannot begin before it becomes available. For every job, its chosen start time must be no earlier than the time at which that job is released.

description: Track how far ahead of schedule each job finishes. For every job, the time it starts plus the time it takes to process plus the amount by which it beats its deadline must equal that job's due date.

description: The makespan represents when the very last job is done. For every job, the makespan must be at least the time at which that job finishes, meaning its start time plus its processing duration.

description: When two jobs share the same machine and the first of the pair is sequenced ahead of the second, the second cannot start until the first has finished. For each machine and each pair of jobs, enforce that the first job's completion time is no later than the second job's start time, but allow this requirement to switch off whenever the two jobs are not both on that machine or the first is not the one running earlier. Use a large enough slack so that any one of those off conditions fully relaxes the requirement.

description: This is the mirror image of the previous sequencing requirement, covering the case where the second job of the pair runs before the first. When two jobs share the same machine and the second of the pair is sequenced ahead of the first, the first cannot start until the second has finished. For each machine and each pair of jobs, enforce that the second job's completion time is no later than the first job's start time, but allow this requirement to switch off whenever the two jobs are not both on that machine or the second is not the one running earlier. Use a large enough slack so that any one of those off conditions fully relaxes the requirement.

vars.name: y

description: To build the complete model, enforce the following relationships in order. First, each job cannot begin before it becomes available. For every job, its chosen start time must be no earlier than the time at which that job is released. Second, track how far ahead of schedule each job finishes. For every job, the time it starts plus the time it takes to process plus the amount by which it beats its deadline must equal that job's due date. Third, every job has to run on a machine, and only one. For each job, the number of machines it is assigned to must be exactly one. Fourth, the makespan represents when the very last job is done. For every job, the makespan must be at least the time at which that job finishes, meaning its start time plus its processing duration. Fifth, when two jobs share the same machine and the first of the pair is sequenced ahead of the second, the second cannot start until the first has finished. For each machine and each pair of jobs, enforce that the first job's completion time is no later than the second job's start time, but allow this requirement to switch off whenever the two jobs are not both on that machine or the first is not the one running earlier. Use a large enough slack so that any one of those off conditions fully relaxes the requirement. Finally, this is the mirror image of the previous sequencing requirement, covering the case where the second job of the pair runs before the first. When two jobs share the same machine and the second of the pair is sequenced ahead of the first, the first cannot start until the second has finished. For each machine and each pair of jobs, enforce that the second job's completion time is no later than the first job's start time, but allow this requirement to switch off whenever the two jobs are not both on that machine or the second is not the one running earlier. Use a large enough slack so that any one of those off conditions fully relaxes the requirement.
